coredata.dc:title: Free regulation of luminous color of Ln-CQDs synthesized with citric acid-chelated Ln ions as the precursor
coredata.dc:creator: He, Huan
Ma, Yue
Li, Junfeng
Lai, Xuefei
Chen, Xianfei
Wang, Li
Zhang, Wentao
Huang, Yi
Zhang, Peicong
coredata.dc:description: In order to avoid the shortcoming of the luminescence singleness of traditional carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the free regulation of luminous color of CQDs were achieved by doping lanthanide ions in the study. Firstly, Eu3+-doped carbon quantum dots (Eu-CQDs) were synthesized with citric acid-chelated europium ions as the precursor by a hydrothermal method. Doped europium ions existed in the Eu(III) state in Eu-CQDs and the prepared Eu-CQDs showed two parts of the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum, which originated from the blue emission of CQDs and the characteristic photoluminescence of Eu3+ ions, respectively. With an increase in the concentration of Eu3+, the intensity of Eu3+ characteristic emission showed an enhanced trend, but the intensity of the characteristic peaks of CQDs decreased. The CIE coordinate confirmed the continuous regulation of the luminous color of CQDs from blue to red. When the concentration of Eu3+ ions was 6%, the ratio of PL intensity between Eu3+ ions and CQDs was higher, thus generating purple-red light emission. In addition, the dual fluorescent characteristics of Eu-CQDs showed quite different responses to different environment pH values. When pH = 6, the CCT value of Eu(6%)-CQDs was 2156 K, indicating a good red light performance. The white light emission of CQDs could be realized by co-doping 5 mol% Eu3+ and 2 mol% Tb3+ in CQDs and adjusting the environmental pH = 7. The CCT value of doped CQDs was 5115 K, indicating a good neutral white light performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doping lanthanide ions
Carbon quantum dot
Luminous color
Free regulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of Ln-CQDs at different environmental pH
Free regulation of luminous color of Ln-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of CA-CQDs (A, B) and Eu(6%)-CQDs (C, D).

Structural characterization of CA-CQDs and Eu-CQDs. A: XRD patterns of CA-CQDs and Eu-CQDs (x = 3%, 5%, 6%) B: Raman spectra of CA-CQDs and Eu(x%)-CQDs (x = 3%, 4%, 6%) C: IR spectra of CA-CQDs and Eu(x%)-CQDs (x = 3%, 6%) D: EDS of Eu(3%)-CQDs.
XPS (A), C1s (B), O1s (C), and Eu3d (D) of Eu(3%)-CQDs.

Optical properties of Eu(3%)-CQDs. A: Excitation spectrum of Eu(3%)-CQDs at emission wavelength of 420 and 621 nm; B: Emission spectra of the Eu(3%)-CQDs under the excitation of different wavelengths (300–393 nm).
Emission spectra of the Eu(x)-CQDs(x = 0–6%) under the excitation of 320 nm. Inset: Intensity curve of Eu(x)-CQDs(x = 0–6%) under the excitation of 320 nm and the emission of 423 nm.

Emission spectra of the Eu(x)-CQDs(x = 1–6%) under the excitation of 393 nm. Inset shows the linear relationship between the concentration of Eu3+ ions and the ratio of PL intensity between Eu3+ ions and CQDs as well as the CIE chromaticity diagram of luminescence color of Eu(x)-CQDs(x = 1–6%). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Emission spectra of Eu(6%)-CQDs under different environmental pH values. The top left inset shows the CIE chromaticity diagram of Eu(6%)-CQDs under different environmental pH values.

Emission spectra of Tb3+, Eu3+ co-doped CQDs with 5% mol Eu3+ and 2% mol Tb3+ under different environmental pH values. The inset at the lower right corner shows the CIE coordinates of CQDs under three different pH values.
CIE parameters of the Eu(6%)-CQDs under different environmental pH.

head.ce:title: Free regulation of luminous color of Ln-CQDs synthesized with citric acid-chelated Ln ions as the precursor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials, Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu, 610059, Sichuan, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials, Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, Sichuan, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, Sichuan, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu, 610059, Sichuan, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, Sichuan, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to avoid the shortcoming of the luminescence singleness of traditional carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the free regulation of luminous color of CQDs were achieved by doping lanthanide ions in the study. Firstly, Eu3+-doped carbon quantum dots (Eu-CQDs) were synthesized with citric acid-chelated europium ions as the precursor by a hydrothermal method. Doped europium ions existed in the Eu(III) state in Eu-CQDs and the prepared Eu-CQDs showed two parts of the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum, which originated from the blue emission of CQDs and the characteristic photoluminescence of Eu3+ ions, respectively. With an increase in the concentration of Eu3+, the intensity of Eu3+ characteristic emission showed an enhanced trend, but the intensity of the characteristic peaks of CQDs decreased. The CIE coordinate confirmed the continuous regulation of the luminous color of CQDs from blue to red. When the concentration of Eu3+ ions was 6%, the ratio of PL intensity between Eu3+ ions and CQDs was higher, thus generating purple-red light emission. In addition, the dual fluorescent characteristics of Eu-CQDs showed quite different responses to different environment pH values. When pH = 6, the CCT value of Eu(6%)-CQDs was 2156 K, indicating a good red light performance. The white light emission of CQDs could be realized by co-doping 5 mol% Eu3+ and 2 mol% Tb3+ in CQDs and adjusting the environmental pH = 7. The CCT value of doped CQDs was 5115 K, indicating a good neutral white light performance.

ce:section.ce:para: As a promising photoluminescent nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much attention in recent years. CQDs have been applied in sensing and imaging due to their low toxicity, biocompatibility, high chemical stability, tunable electronic properties and ease of synthesis and surface modification [1–3]. However, CQDs show the excitation-dependent emission behavior, the dominated blue and green light emission, thus limiting their applications [5–7]. Especially in organisms, under ultraviolet-blue light excitation, tissues often give blue light emission, which strongly interferes with the recognition of CQDs. In addition, multicolor luminescent carbon quantum dots are also required for display. Hence, it is necessary to develop an appropriate method to regulate the color of carbon quantum dots [8–11].

With the rich electronic energy level and energy level transition path, Lanthanide ions are widely used as luminescent centers in the development of multicolor luminescent materials [12–17]. Hence, the preparation of color-adjustable CQDs-based hybrid materials through the combination CQDs and lanthanide ions has become a hotspot. In 2014, Dong et al. [18] introduced europium nitrate into the PEG400 solution and synthesized Eu-CQDs with the improved red light emission by pyrolysis. In 2016, Zhang et al. [19] prepared the white light emission CQDs by co-doping Tb3+ and Eu3+ in ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid tetrasodium salt precursor. The luminous colors of the above samples were all changed by introducing lanthanide ions, but the free regulation of the luminous color of the CQDs has not been reported [20–22].

Aiming at the defects of the above-mentioned lanthanide ions-doped carbon quantum dots (Ln-CQDs), the idea of freely regulating the luminous color of CQDs by adjusting the doping ratio of lanthanide ions has been proposed. Among all lanthanide ions, Tb3+ and Eu3+ are the advantageous choice for luminous green and red emitters. In this work, a small amounts of Eu3+/Tb3+ were introduced in citric acid matrix. Firstly, citric acid was combined with lanthanide ions to form chelates and then Ln-CQDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. The continuous regulating of the luminous color of the CQDs from blue to red could be achieved by adjusting the doping amounts of Eu3+ and Tb3+. In addition, this paper also discussed the relationship between environmental pH and Förster resonance energy transfer efficiency of CQDs and lanthanide ions. The luminous color of carbon quantum dots could be further regulated by adjusting the environmental pH. In summary, Ln-CQDs with luminous color modulation capacity have the application potential in biological imaging, sensing, display and identification.

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, 99.5%) and concentrated nitric acid (HNO3, AR) was purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd., Chengdu, China. Europium oxide (Eu2O3, 99.9%) and Terbium Oxide (Tb4O7, 99.99%) was bought from Haiyingyuan Chemical Co., Ltd. Distilled water was used in the experiment and all the chemicals were used without further puriﬁcation.

Eu-CQDs were prepared based on previous reports [23–25], but with some modification. Specifically, Eu-CQDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method with europium citrate chelate as the precursor. Firstly, Solution I was prepared by dissolving 0.01 mol citric acid in 30 mL of distilled water at room temperature. The Eu(NO3)3 solution was prepared by dissolving stoichiometric amounts of Eu2O3 in 0.1 mL of HNO3 at an elevated temperature and used as Solution II. The molar mass of doped Eu2O3 was calculated based on 0.01 mol citric acid.

Solution I and Solution II were mixed together under constant stirring for 10 min. The homogeneous solution was transferred into a 100.0-mL stainless steel reactor with teﬂon liner for the immediate hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 2 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained pale yellow product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles and the supernatant was collected. Afterwards, the supernatant was transferred to a 10-mL dialysis kit (MWCO 500) and dialyzed in 1.5 L of distilled water for 48 h. In the dialysis process, distilled water was regularly replaced for eight times. Finally, the Eu-CQDs solution was obtained after removing the fluid from the dialysis bag.
Tb-CQDs and Eu3+, Tb3+ co-doped CQDs were synthesized through the above same hydrothermal carbonization process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Ln-CQDs at different environmental pH
ce:section.ce:para: Preparing several Ln-CQDs solutions with the same concentration, different amounts of NaOH were added separately, and the pH of the solution was tested after stirring for 5 min. Fluorescence intensity tests were then performed on each Ln-CQDs solution with different pH values.

Crystal structures were analyzed by a DX-2700 X-ray diffractometer (Dandong Fangyuan Instrument Co., Ltd.) with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 40 mA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope under an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed using TENSOP-27 infrared spectrometer (Bruker Optics). Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw plus laser Raman spectrometer with λexc = 785 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) patterns were measured on a thermoelectron instrument (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB250) with Al Ka X-ray radiation as the excitation source (1486.8 eV, 500 mm). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Lambda 950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The luminescence spectra were measured with a ﬂuorescence spectrometer (FL-4600, Hitachi). All the measurements were performed at room temperature.

The CA-CQDs and Eu (6%)-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, black dots are the as-synthesized CA-CQDs with the size range of 1.0 nm–4.0 nm and an average diameter of 1.5 nm. The HRTEM image shows the lattice plane with an interfering distance of 0.252 nm, which corresponded to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon. When Eu3+ concentration increased to 6% (Fig. 1C and D), a better and more complete structure had been developed and the size of the as-prepared Eu(6%)-CQDs became larger (average diameter, 5 nm). The HRTEM image shows the lattice distance of 0.188 nm, which corresponded to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. It was indicated that a small amount of europium ions would not destroy the matrix structure of carbon quantum dots. The increased size might be ascribed to the capturing function of Eu3+ ions. The volume of Eu(6%)-CQDs was increased by the introduction of Eu3+ ion, thus resulting in the relatively larger particle size.

In order to further analyze the structure and the degree of disorder of Eu-CQDs, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and Raman spectra of CA-CQDs and Eu(3%)-CQDs were obtained. Fig. 2 A shows the XRD patterns of CA-CQDs and Eu(3%)-CQDs. The as-prepared CA-CQDs belonged to the amorphous phase and two steamed bread peaks observed at 26° and 44° corresponded to the (002) and (101) planes of graphene carbon, respectively. When the europium ion concentration increased to 3%, the XRD patterns of Eu(3%)-CQDs showed no significant difference compared to those of undoped CQDs. Continue to increase europium concentration to 5% or 6%, the continuity of the graphite structure of CQDs was affected and the ordering is reduced. It could be concluded that the orderly of carbon dots is deteriorated after the introduction of europium ions.

Fig. 2B shows the Raman spectra of CA-CQDs and Eu(3%)-CQDs. The D-band at 1334.3 cm−1 was ascribed to sp3 carbon, whereas the G-band at 1642.1 cm−1 was ascribed to sp2 carbons. The D peak of CA-CQDs was broadened and the intensity of the D-band and G-band was approximately the same, indicating the high degree of disorder of carbon quantum dots. After the introduction of europium ions, when Eu3+ concentration increased to 3% and 4%, the intensity ratio of D-band to G-band was decreased, thus resulting in a decrease in the degree of disorder. When Eu3+ doping concentration was 6%, the intensity ratio of D-band to G-band was increased. The reason might be interpreted as follows. Due to the capturing effect of Eu3+ on citric acid, the better and more complete structure could be developed when Eu3+ doping concentration was relatively small, thus improving the degree of order of Eu-CQDs. When Eu3+ doping concentration was excessive, the structure of carbon quantum dots may be destroyed to a certain extent, and then causing the high degree of disorder.

The FTIR spectra of CA-CQDs, Eu(3%)-CQDs and Eu(6%)-CQDs were shown in Fig. 2C. The spectra of CA-CQDs and Eu(3%)-CQDs and Eu(6%)-CQDs were very similar. The peak at 3421 cm−1 was the stretching O–H vibrations, 2800–3000 cm−1 to the C–H stretching bands. The peaks at 1585 and 1412 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of COO−, respectively. The band 1078–1259 cm−1 was assigned to aliphatic C–H vibrations. As for Eu-CQDs, there were some difference comparing to the CA-CQDs. The peak at about 1585 cm−1 was strengthened and narrowed, the peak at about 1412 cm−1 differentiates into two peaks, indicating the interaction among Eu3+ with the surface groups including CO, –OH.
The EDS spectra of the Eu(3%)-CQDs indicated that the elemental composition of Eu(3%)-CQDs was C (48.10%), O (45.23%), and Eu (6.67%).

The XPS spectra of Eu(3%)-CQDs were obtained to analyze the surface element composition and chemical states of europium ions-doped CQDs. Three strong peaks at 1154.08, 530.08, and 284.08 eV were respectively ascribed to Eu3d, O1s, and C1s (Fig. 3 A). The deconvoluted C1s binding energy spectrum (Fig. 3B) was fitted into four peaks at around 288.56 eV, 285.92 eV and 284.78 eV, which were respectively attributed to CO, C–OH and CC. The O1s binding energy spectrum of Eu(3%)-CQDs was deconvoluted into two main peaks centered at 532.66 eV and 531.4 eV, which were respectively ascribed to C–OH (or C–O–C) and CO (Fig. 3C). High resolution Eu3d binding energy spectrum of the Eu(3%)-CQDs is shown in 3D. Four identical peaks were observed at 1124.64, 1134.6, 1154.04 and 1164.48 eV. The former three peaks were ascribed to Eu-3d5/2 and the last peak at 1164.48 eV was ascribed to Eu-3d3/2. The results were well consistent with those of Eu2O3, indicating that doped Eu existed in the Eu(III) state in Eu-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free regulation of luminous color of Ln-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to display the effect of the introduction of Eu3+ on the fluorescence intensity of Eu-CQDs, the fluorescence properties of Eu(3%)-CQDs were further discussed. Fig. 4 A presents the excitation spectra of Eu(3%)-CQDs under the emission wavelength of 420 and 622 nm. In the excitation spectra, a wide peak in the wavelength range of 250–400 nm at an emission wavelength of 420 nm was ascribed to the characteristic excitation of CQDs. When the monitored wavelength was 621 nm, in addition to the characteristic peaks of CQDs, a series of sharp peaks corresponding to the intra-conﬁgurational f–f transition of Eu3+ ions were observed in the range of 350–550 nm. The transitions are indexed as: (7F0→5L8) at 361 nm, (7F0→5G3 5L7) at 380 nm, (7F0→5L6) at 393 nm, (7F0→5D2) at 464 nm, and (7F0→5D1) at 536 nm [26].

Fig. 4b shows the emission spectra under the excitation of 300–393 nm. The as-prepared Eu(3%)-CQDs showed excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission and the maximum emission was available at 425 nm under the maximum excitation at 320 nm. The slight difference in the emission spectrum of Eu-CQDs and CA-CQDs are shown in right inset of Fig. 4b. After the introduction of Eu3+ ions, several weak narrow peaks occurred in the range of 570–725 nm and could be ascribed to the emission bands of Eu3+ ions, and the transitions were indexed as 5D0−7F1 (around 590 nm), 5D0−7F2 (around 621 nm), and 5D0−7F4 (around 705 nm) transitions [27]. Therefore, it was proved that Eu3+ ions successfully entered the CQDs, which exhibited the characteristic emission of Eu3+ ions under ultraviolet light excitation.

We further evaluated the influences of different Eu3+ concentrations on the fluorescent properties of the Eu-CQDs. Fig. 5 shows the emission spectra of the Eu(x)-CQDs(x = 0–6%) under the excitation of 320 nm. When Eu3+ concentration was 1%, the characteristic emission intensity of CQDs greatly increased compared with CA-CQDs, indicating that the synthesis efficiency of CQDs was improved to some degree due to the capturing effect of europium ions on citrate acid. When Eu3+ doping concentration further increased, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-CQDs showed a decreasing trend due to the existence of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from CQDs to Eu3+ ions [28,29].

In order to clarify the effect of Eu3+ concentration on the fluorescence intensity of Eu-CQDs, the emission spectra of the Eu(x)-CQDs(x = 1–6%) under the excitation of 393 nm were explored (Fig. 6 ). The conspicuous characteristic emission spectra of Eu3+ ion were observed under the excitation of 393 nm. With the increase in europium ion concentration, the characteristic emission of Eu3+ ions was gradually enhanced but the intensity of the characteristic peaks of CQDs decreased, demonstrating another FRET from CQDs to Eu3+ ions.

The inset in Fig. 6 shows the linear relationship between Eu3+ ion concentration and the ratio of PL intensity between Eu3+ ions and CQDs. When the Eu3+ ion concentration increased from 1% to 3%, the ratio of PL intensity between Eu3+ ions and CQDs increased below 1. Eu(x)-CQDs(x = 1–3%) all exhibited blue emission, as indicated in CIE coordinate analysis. When Eu3+ concentration increased from 4% to 6%, the ratio of PL intensity between Eu3+ ions and CQDs was increased above 1. The red light emission became dominant and the CIE coordinates were constantly redshifted with the increase in Eu3+ ion concentration. Eu(4%)-CQDs showed blue emission but with a hint of red and Eu(5%)-CQDs exhibited purple emission. When Eu3+ ion concentration was 6%, the CIE coordinates were located in the purple-red light region.

Fig. 7 shows the emission spectra of Eu(6%)-CQDs under different environmental pH values. The two characteristic peaks of Eu(6%)-CQDs had different responses to different environmental pH values. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs showed smaller changes, but the luminescence intensity of Eu3+ ions increased greatly with the rise of pH value, indicating the enhanced Förster resonance energy transfer effect from CQDs to Eu3+ ions. The reason may be interpreted as follows. Due to the protonation effect of surface groups of CQDs in the acidic environment, the combination force between Eu3+ ions and molecular groups was weakened [30]. When the environmental pH value of the CQDs solution increased, the bonding strength between carbon dots and Eu3+ ions would be enhanced because of the deprotonation effect, thus promoting the Förster resonance energy transfer from CQDs to Eu3+ ions.

The top left inset of Fig. 7 shows the CIE chromaticity diagram of Eu(6%)-CQDs under different environmental pH values. When pH increased from 2 to 6, the CIE chromaticity of Eu(6%)-CQDs was redshifted. The highest purity red occurred when pH = 6. When the pH value continued to increase, the CIE chromaticity exhibited green shift and the color turned from red to yellow-red.

Furthermore, the correlated color temperature (CCT) of samples under environmental pH = 4, 6, 7, 9 was calculated as [31]:
(1)
CCT = −
437 n
3
+
3601 n
2
− 6861 n + 5514.31 ,
where n=(x−xe)/(y−ye); (x, y) is the coordinates of a sample; (xe, ye) is the coordinate of epicenter and its value is xe = 0.3320 and ye = 0.1858. When pH value increased from 4 to 6, the CCT value of Eu(6%)-CQDs varied from 1781 to 2156 K; when pH value increased from 6 to 9, the CCT value showed a downward trend (Table 1 ). When pH = 6, the CCT value of Eu(6%)-CQDs was 2156 K, indicating a good red light performance.

White light emitting materials are usually composed of three primary colors, which are blue, green, and red. The prepared Eu(x)-CQDs could afford blue and red light emission, so the white light emitting CQDs would be obtained if the missing green light could be compensated.

Fig. 8 shows the emission spectra of Tb3+ and Eu3+ co-doped CQDs with 5 mol% Eu3+ and 2 mol% Tb3+ under the excitation of 380 nm. In order to adjust the ratio of three characteristic peaks, the effects of different environmental pH values (pH = 2, 4, and 7) on Tb3+, Eu3+ co-doped CQDs were discussed. For simplicity, the samples with pH = 2, 4, and 7 were named Samples A, B and C, respectively. Three parts of the PL spectrum (425–485 nm, 485–575 nm and 575–725 nm) originated from the blue light emission of the CQDs, green light of Tb3+ and red light of Eu3+ ions (Fig. 8). With the increase in environmental pH value, the emission of Tb3+ and Eu3+ ions greatly increased, whereas the intensity of characteristic peaks of CQDs slightly decreased. In order to more clearly display the color of the three samples, the related CIE coordinates are shown in Fig. 8. When pH = 2, the coordinates of Sample A was (0.22, 0.20), and Sample A gave pale blue light. With increase in pH value, the coordinates of Sample B shifted to (0.29, 0.24), indicating pale purple emission. When pH increased to 7, the coordinates of Sample C shifted to (0.34, 0.32), indicating white light emission. In addition, the CCT value of 2%Tb3+, 5%Eu3+ co-doped CQDs at environmental pH = 7 was calculated to be 5115 K, indicating a good neutral white light performance.

In this study, Eu3+/Tb3+-doped CQDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method, and the free regulation of luminous color of CQDs was achieved by adjusting the doping ratio of Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions and the environmental pH.

With the increase in Eu3+ concentration, the size of Eu-CQDs increased. When Eu3+ ion concentration increased to 6%, the average size of Eu-CQDs grew up to 5 nm, but the size of CA-CQDs was just 1.5 nm. With the increase in europium ion concentration, the characteristic emission of Eu3+ ions showed an enhanced trend, but the intensity of the characteristic peaks of CQDs decreased and the continuous regulation of luminous color of CQDs from blue to red was achieved successfully. When Eu3+ ion concentration was 6%, the ratio of PL intensity between Eu3+ ions and CQDs was higher and the CIE coordinate of Eu(6%)-CQDs was located in the purple-red light region. Moreover, the two characteristic peaks of the prepared Eu-CQDs showed different responses to different environmental pH values. When pH = 6, the CCT value of Eu(6%)-CQDs was 2156 K, indicating a good red light performance. In this way, the white light emission of CQDs could be realized by co-doping 5 mol% Eu3+ and 2 mol% Tb3+ in CQDs and adjusting the environmental pH = 7. The CCT value of the prepared CQDs:5%Eu3+,2%Tb3+ was 5115 K, indicating a good neutral white light performance.

Huan He: Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yue Ma: Data curation, Formal analysis. Junfeng Li: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Xianfei Chen: Investigation. Li Wang: Investigation. Wentao Zhang: Resources. Yi Huang: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was jointly supported by the Science and Technology Innovation Miaozi Project of Sichuan Province (2018035), National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 41673109), the Science and Technology Department Project of Sichuan Province (2017SZ0185, 2018SZDZX0022), and Science and Technology Research Strategic Resource Innovation and Development Pilot Zone of Sichuan Panxi (CDWA2016ZC3-1).
